w:document.mc:Ignorable: w14 w15 w16se w16cid w16 w16cex w16sdtdh wp14

w:r.w:t: : An AI-Powered Multi-Source Academic Notification Intelligence System for Pakistani University Students
is a smart student productivity platform that solves one of the most frustrating daily problems Pakistani university students
— scattered academic communication across multiple platforms. Instead of jumping between WhatsApp groups, Gmail, and Google Classroom constantly,

connects all three, reads everything automatically, understands what each message is about using AI, organizes it into a clean dashboard, and lets students interact with all their academic data through a natural language chatbot that can both answer questions and take actions on their behalf.

The system is built around a fine-tuned multilingual AI classifier that understands both English and Roman Urdu — the way Pakistani students actually communicate. It extracts deadlines from natural language, scores urgency dynamically, maps messages to the right courses, and alerts students before they miss anything important.
Pakistani university students deal with a genuinely chaotic academic communication situation every single day. There is no standard — every professor does things differently:
Professor A posts assignments only on Google Classroom
Professor B sends deadline updates through WhatsApp group
Professor C communicates exclusively through email
The CR (class representative) posts in a general WhatsApp group on behalf of multiple professors
Society groups share event announcements mixed in with casual conversation
Some professors announce things verbally in class and nowhere else

The result is that students are constantly context-switching between apps, missing notifications buried in 200 unread WhatsApp messages, forgetting deadlines that were announced casually in a group chat, and having no single place to see what is actually due this week.
What makes this worse for Pakistani students specifically is that communication happens in Roman Urdu —

existing tool understands or processes. Tools like Eventbrite, Google Classroom, or generic reminder apps either don't work across multiple platforms or don't understand how Pakistani students and professors actually communicate.
solves exactly this — one unified intelligent system that reads everything, understands Roman Urdu, organizes automatically, and never lets a student miss something important.
At onboarding, student chooses which platforms to connect — not everyone uses all three, and different universities have different styles:
via Baileys library — student picks exactly which groups to monitor. Two types of groups: academic subject groups and society/community announcement groups
via Google Gmail API — reads academic emails, exam date sheets, semester calendars, professor emails
via Google Classroom API — reads announcements, assignments, and materials
All three are optional. Student connects whatever their university actually uses.
Every notification needs to know which course it belongs to. This is handled through a combination of student setup and AI assistance:
"My OS WhatsApp group and CS301 on Classroom are both for Operating Systems"
Multiple sources can map to the same course — WhatsApp group + Classroom + email thread all tagged as one course
For general class groups where the CR posts for multiple subjects — AI reads the message and tries to identify the course. If confident → tags automatically. If ambiguous → asks student:
"This message seems to be about Operating Systems — is that correct?"
Student confirms or corrects → system remembers permanently
LLM already knows common Pakistani university short forms — DL = Deep Learning, NLP = Natural Language Processing, DSA = Data Structures, OS = Operating Systems, DB
= Database, OOP = Object Oriented Programming. For ones it doesn't know → asks student once → stores in a personal abbreviation dictionary for that student. Never asks again.
Every notification is tagged with both a
) — two independent tags that make filtering powerful.
Every incoming message from any source goes through a fine-tuned multilingual transformer model that classifies it into one of these categories:
Collect 300-500 real WhatsApp messages from university groups manually
Label each one with the correct category
) on this labeled dataset using Google
Model runs on laptop CPU during actual use — fast enough at 0.2 seconds per message
This is the genuine AI/ML research contribution of the project.
After classification, if a message is Assignment, Quiz, or Exam Schedule — it gets sent to the LLM (
, free tier) which extracts any deadline mentioned:
Handles English and Roman Urdu naturally in one unified system. No separate rule-based system, no
library — just the LLM. Clean and simple.
Pure Python logic — no LLM needed here. Runs every time the dashboard loads or every few hours in background. Compares current time against extracted deadline:
Student marks an item done → urgency removed
item moves to completed. Item not marked done as deadline approaches → urgency escalates automatically without student doing anything.
— triggered for High and Critical urgency items. Native OS notification using plyer library
— system sends a message to student's own WhatsApp (saved as a contact) for Critical items:
Critical: OS Assignment due in 6 hours — submit karo
— every morning a scheduled summary sent to student's WhatsApp:
ka schedule: 2 deadlines, 1 quiz, 3 pending assignments"
Urgency escalates silently → when threshold crossed (e.g. moves from Medium to High) → notification triggered automatically
genuinely different from a simple notification aggregator. The chatbot has full access to the student's complete database and can both answer questions AND take actions.
→ lists all pending deadlines this week with urgency levels
→ finds most recent material tagged as NLP course
→ counts all pending items across all courses
"Add NLP assignment, topic word embeddings, deadline Sunday
→ creates notification entry in database immediately
"Add Monday 9am to 11am CS301 Operating Systems Room 204 to my timetable"
"Map my DL WhatsApp group to Deep Learning course"
"Delete the quiz I manually added yesterday"
"Remind me about the DB project, it's due next Saturday"
Confirmation required before any delete action. Everything else executes immediately and confirms back:
"Done — NLP assignment added, deadline Sunday 11:59 PM."

Student's entire semester database (all notifications, timetable, course mappings, urgency scores) is loaded into the LLM's context as structured JSON. For 3-4 students, this is approximately 5,000-8,000 tokens — well within

. LLM reads the data, understands the question or instruction, either answers directly or calls the appropriate database function (insert/update/delete). Function calling is a standard LLM feature supported natively by most modern models.
Always available regardless of chatbot. Simple form in the UI:
Type: Assignment / Quiz / Announcement / Event / Exam Schedule

Tagged as "manually added" with a subtle indicator. Treated exactly like auto-detected notifications everywhere in the system. This covers the very real Pakistani university scenario where a professor announces something verbally in class only.
Today's urgent notifications at the top, sorted by urgency score. This week's upcoming deadlines below. Clean at-a-glance view — student opens app and immediately knows what needs attention today.

All assignments and quizzes from all sources and all courses. Filters: by course (dropdown), by status (pending / done / overdue), by urgency level. Each item shows: course badge, type badge, description, deadline, urgency indicator, mark-as-done button. Default sort: most urgent first.
All class cancellations, venue changes, schedule updates, general academic notices. Filtered by course. Simple list, no urgency scoring needed here — announcements are informational.

All shared lecture slides, notes, PDFs, links organized by course. For Classroom and Email sources — shows direct link to open. For WhatsApp sources — file auto-downloaded to a local folder organized as: /
/filename. Student never manually saves a shared file again.

Messages from society and community groups classified as events. Shown exactly as they appeared in WhatsApp — raw message text, no processing, no extraction. Simple "Event" badge as heading. Sorted by date received. Student reads the full message themselves to get registration deadlines, event dates, venue etc. No sub-categories, no further AI processing.

Manual entry — student fills in their weekly schedule: day, time, subject/course, room number. Clean weekly grid view. Used internally by chatbot to answer schedule questions and contextualize notifications.
Step by step when student first opens the app:
Select which platforms to connect (checkboxes — WhatsApp / Gmail / Classroom)
Connect selected platforms — QR code scan for WhatsApp (just like WhatsApp Web), Google OAuth login for Gmail and Classroom
From WhatsApp — see list of all groups, student ticks which ones to monitor (subject groups separately from society groups)
Map groups and classrooms to courses — either via simple UI form or by telling chatbot

End of semester — one button. All current notifications, mappings, timetable archived with a semester label (e.g. "Fall 2025"). Student can browse archived semesters anytime. Fresh onboarding starts for new semester — new groups, new courses, new timetable. Clean slate, zero clutter.
Development Workflow (How You Will Actually Build This)

Set up project structure. Get WhatsApp connection working via Baileys — this is the most important and fiddly piece. Get a test message flowing from WhatsApp group into your backend. Set up PostgreSQL database with basic schema. Basic React frontend shell.
Collect and label 300-500 WhatsApp messages dataset. Fine-tune XLM-
. Test classification on real messages. Set up
API for deadline extraction. Test on Roman Urdu messages.

Google OAuth setup. Gmail API integration — read emails, extract body. Classroom API — read announcements and assignments. All messages from all three sources now flowing through the same classifier pipeline.
Build all tabs in React. Urgency scoring logic. Course mapping UI. Manual add notification form. Basic filtering and sorting.
Build agentic chatbot with full database context. Implement function calling for CRUD actions. Test with Roman Urdu queries. Connect to dashboard.
Desktop notifications via plyer. WhatsApp self-message via Baileys. Daily morning digest
job. Onboarding flow. Semester reset. UI polish.
End to end testing with real usage. Fix edge cases. Prepare demo script. Simulate a full semester's worth of data for convincing demo.
User Workflow (How a Student Actually Uses It)

→ selects WhatsApp + Gmail + Classroom → scans QR code with phone → logs into Google → picks 4 WhatsApp groups to monitor (OS group, NLP group, DB group, university announcements group) → tells chatbot:
Opens app → Home tab shows today's priorities → sees OS assignment due tomorrow (
Critical) → clicks it → reads details → marks as done after submitting → chatbot in morning already sent WhatsApp digest.
— Assignment 2, topic Transformers, deadline Friday 11:59 PM, urgency High"
Student opens app → taps Manual Add → selects Quiz → selects OS course → types "Chapter 5 se quiz" → sets deadline to next Monday → saved. System now tracks it like any other notification.
Taps Semester Reset → confirms → everything archived under "Fall 2025" → fresh start for Spring 2026.
Direct WebSocket to WhatsApp, no browser needed, lightweight, supports multiple users
Official free API, same OAuth as Gmail
Pre-trained on 100 languages including Urdu, fine-tuned on our dataset, runs on CPU
Free tier, 131k context window, very fast, handles Roman Urdu
3 lines of code, works on Windows/Mac/Linux
Proper relational DB for multi-user data with relationships
Baileys runs in Node, AI pipeline in Python — they talk via HTTP
Events tab display (raw message, no processing)
WhatsApp integration via Baileys (session management, group selection, reconnection)
Assignments and quizzes tab with multi-filter
fine-tuning (data collection, labeling, training, evaluation, integration)
Deadline extraction via LLM (prompt engineering, JSON parsing, edge cases in Roman Urdu)
Course mapping system with abbreviation learning and ambiguity resolution
Agentic chatbot with function calling and full database context
Materials auto-download and local folder organization
️ Challenges and How You Handle Them

WhatsApp does not officially allow bots. Baileys mimics a normal WhatsApp Web session via WebSocket which reduces ban risk. For a student project with 3-4 users running on home internet, the risk is very low. Mitigations: never mark messages as read automatically, poll every few minutes instead of real-time, run on home
not a data-center server, stagger session connections.
Pakistani students write Roman Urdu in wildly inconsistent ways —
all mean tomorrow. The fine-tuned XLM-

handles most variation since it's trained on real messages. The LLM handles deadline extraction edge cases. For anything truly ambiguous — the system asks the student. Asking is not a failure, it's honest design.
Challenge 3 — Course recognition from general groups

When a CR posts for multiple courses in one group, the AI reads the message and tries to identify which course. Short forms like DL, NLP, OS are handled by LLM's existing knowledge. Unknown abbreviations → asks student once → stores permanently. The course mapping system gets smarter over the semester as it builds the student's personal abbreviation dictionary.
Challenge 4 — No historical data for demo
stress predictor was removed. The chatbot and urgency system work from day one with no historical data needed. For a compelling demo, you manually add 2-3
of fake notifications to show the system working with realistic data.

free tier is generous — 14,400 requests per day on Llama 3.1 8B. For 3-4 students using the system, you will never hit this limit. Each message that needs deadline extraction = 1 LLM call. Each chatbot message = 1 LLM call. A student might generate 5-10 messages per day total. That is nowhere near the limit.
Challenge 6 — Keeping Node.js and Python in sync
Baileys runs in Node.js, AI pipeline in Python. They communicate via a simple internal HTTP REST API. Node receives WhatsApp message → POSTs to Python
→ Python classifies, extracts deadline, saves to database → returns result to Node. Clean separation, easy to debug, industry standard pattern.
That is your complete detailed proposal. When you are ready to generate the formal professor-facing version, just say go.
